10 min read
Just now

--

The three structural changes that made my AI/ML teams faster. None of them involved a new AI tool.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Kristie J. Rowley, PhD
July 2026
About the Author: Dr. Rowley is an AI operations strategist and consultant with 30+ years helping leaders build business solutions and navigate technological change. Follow her on LinkedIn and Substack.
A few anecdotes in this piece are composites or have had identifying details changed to protect the privacy of individual team members.

The Real Work of AI Transformation
Six months ago, I made a decision that, at first glance, sounded reckless.
We stopped running sprints. No sprint planning, no backlog grooming, no sprint cycles. Instead, my AI/ML engineering teams now work in a continuous flow model, where projects are defined collaboratively with stakeholders, prioritized continuously, and picked up as soon as they’re ready.
At the time, I wasn’t trying to make a statement about Agile. I was trying to solve a practical problem: our engineering process was becoming slower than our engineers. Like many organizations, we had embraced AI to accelerate development, and our engineers could prototype, iterate, and deliver faster than ever before. What surprised us was where the work started slowing down.
It wasn’t slowing down because engineering couldn’t keep up. It was slowing down because our operating model couldn’t keep up.
That realization led us to rethink much more than sprint planning. Over six months, we came to see that AI was changing our organization in three distinct ways: how work flows, how teams learn, and where engineers create the most value. Looking back, none of the changes we made were really about AI. They were about recognizing that AI had moved where work gets stuck, and redesigning our teams and processes around that new reality.
This is what we’ve learned so far.

When Sprints Stopped Making Sense
Traditional Agile assumes a fairly predictable rhythm of work. Projects can be estimated, dependencies are reasonably well understood, and teams commit to a body of work, execute against it, and inspect and adapt every couple of weeks. For a long time, that rhythm served us well.
Increasingly, our AI/ML work stopped fitting neatly inside those boundaries, and it broke in two ways. First, our sprints were constantly interrupted by dependencies outside our control: a stakeholder decision that hadn’t landed, a data source that wasn’t ready, an approval sitting in someone else’s queue. A two-week box doesn’t absorb that kind of interruption gracefully. It just produces broken sprints and awkward, disappointing retros. Second, and more interesting, AI is fast enough now that two weeks is often too coarse a unit to plan around. We can build and validate things in days that used to take weeks. Sprint planning was, ironically, becoming a drag on our speed rather than a structure for it.
This pattern became impossible to ignore:
AI doesn’t eliminate bottlenecks. It relocates them.
As AI accelerates coding and development, engineering often stops being the limiting factor. The constraints become stakeholder decisions, governance, data readiness, organizational priorities, review cycles. Those can be healthy and necessary constraints. They’re also very different from the ones Agile was originally designed to optimize, and they don’t sit still. They move from project to project, week to week. A fixed sprint boundary doesn’t flex well around a bottleneck that keeps moving.
One project made this especially clear. We built a working prototype in just a few days, far faster than we could have six months earlier. Then it sat for more than a week, waiting on stakeholder decisions and additional data. Under our old operating model, that looked like a sprint that failed to deliver. Under continuous flow, the engineers simply picked up the next ready project while the organization caught up. Nothing was “off track.” The work kept moving instead of waiting.
This isn’t an argument against Agile principles. Collaboration, iteration, and continuous improvement still matter. What changed was the cadence we used to support them. We still plan carefully, prioritize, collaborate closely with stakeholders. What we no longer do is wait for an arbitrary sprint boundary before valuable work can begin.
The skill we’ve had to build — and it is a skill — is getting good at picking work up and putting it down cleanly. When something’s blocked, the team shifts to the next scoped priority instead of stalling. Nobody’s sprint gets hijacked by a dependency, because there’s no sprint boundary to hijack.

Making Time to Learn & Innovate
As AI lets engineers move faster, there’s a temptation to fill every freed-up hour with more delivery. We didn’t do that. We deliberately reserve 10% of every work week for innovation: research, experimentation, learning, trying new tools, exploring ideas that don’t yet belong on a roadmap.
At first glance that seems counterintuitive. If AI increases productivity, why intentionally reduce delivery capacity? Because with AI, today’s best practices rarely stay best practices for long. The tools evolve, the models improve, new techniques emerge almost weekly. If engineers only ever have time to deliver, they eventually stop learning. And in a field moving this quickly, that becomes its own form of technical debt.
The mechanics matter, because innovation time fails when it’s optional. Ours isn’t. Every engineer proposes or is assigned a learning objective, leadership approves it, and it’s tracked through our normal project management process. It’s not a side project floating outside the system, but a real line item. Once a month, everyone demonstrates what they learned to the rest of the team.
That monthly cadence turned out to be the load-bearing piece. It creates a natural forcing function for follow-through, and it turns individual exploration into shared knowledge. One engineer’s afternoon spent evaluating a new framework or an agentic pattern becomes everyone else’s shortcut. That accountability is exactly what makes leadership comfortable protecting the time.
People sometimes hear “10% innovation time” and imagine spare capacity or a team that isn’t busy enough. The reality is the opposite. We didn’t discover spare capacity; we deliberately created it, investing in future capability the same way we invest in paying down technical debt. That meant consciously saying no to some work today so we could do better work tomorrow.
Earlier this year, one engineer used innovation time to test new approaches to one of our RAG pipelines. This work likely never would have happened inside a normal sprint because it had no clear deliverable and no guaranteed payoff. However, it became foundational to much of our generative AI engineering, and most projects we ship now incorporate techniques that originated during innovation time.
It’s not time off from real work. It is real work. It’s just work that doesn’t have to justify itself against this quarter’s roadmap.

Beyond Coding: Engineers as Technical Consultants
This was the biggest cultural shift of the three, and honestly, it was the one I was least sure would land.
For years, technical excellence was measured by how effectively someone could code, develop, implement, and optimize software. Those skills still matter. But as implementation becomes faster and more accessible, the differentiator is no longer just writing code. It’s understanding the problem well enough to build the right solution — and that understanding rarely comes from reading a list of requirements. It comes from partnering directly with the business.
So we moved developers out from behind product and project managers and put them in front of stakeholders. We coached them deliberately, not by osmosis: how to communicate with stakeholders, how to switch between technical and non-technical framing depending on the room, how to run a stakeholder-facing meeting, how to tell when a project needs a check-in and when it doesn’t. They became partners to the business, not a resource behind a request queue.
For engineering teams, this was a big ask. Plenty of engineers didn’t sign up to be client-facing, and not everyone was comfortable at first. But the response from stakeholders has been genuinely enthusiastic. One engineer who used to route every question through a PM now runs a standing weekly meeting with a core group of stakeholders. Decisions that used to take several meetings to clarify requirements, validate constraints, and set timelines now happen in ten minutes. The stakeholders rave about that engineer’s knowledge and patience. Watching that confidence develop has been one of the most rewarding outcomes of the whole experiment.
Beyond steed and effectiveness, there’s a second reason I pushed this change. I’ve noticed it in hiring, too. The strongest candidates aren’t simply the strongest programmers. They’re the ones who understand a business problem deeply enough to frame it well and can explain a technical tradeoff differently to an executive than to another engineer. When I ask a candidate to walk me through something they’ve built, the best ones start with the business problem, explain the tradeoffs they weighed, and land on why they chose what they did. The code is an important part of the story, but it isn’t the story itself.
The same is true inside our organization. The engineers who create the greatest impact aren’t necessarily the ones who write code the fastest or the most elegantly. They’re the ones who consistently connect business problems to practical AI and machine learning solutions and can help others understand those connections. As AI takes over more of the programming, the premium shifts toward exactly that skill: deciding what should be built, and communicating why it’s important.
None of this diminishes deep technical expertise. If anything, it raises the bar. Our engineers still need to understand the underlying technology, evaluate architectures, and make sound engineering decisions. What’s changed is that technical excellence alone is no longer sufficient. The greatest impact now comes from pairing strong engineering fundamentals with a real understanding of the business problem. The closer engineers are to the problem, the better the solutions they build.
I’ll add one caveat: This shift may not be right for every team or every engineer. Some of the best builders I know have no interest in facilitating a stakeholder meeting, and that’s fine. The goal is to make sure this capability exists on the team and is valued when it shows up.

What Actually Changed
At first glance, these look like three unrelated decisions: eliminate sprints, protect innovation time, and develop technical consultants. They weren’t. Each was a response to the same realization: AI had changed the constraints of engineering work, and our operating model hadn’t caught up. Once we saw that, the individual changes became obvious.
Here’s the honest scorecard at the midpoint of the year:

• We spend more time doing the work than planning for it. Charters and scoping documents come together faster now that they’re built with stakeholders up front instead of routed through a planning cycle. We often start development the same week a project is scoped, instead of letting it sit in a backlog for an undefined stretch.

• Velocity is up. Getting blocked no longer derails a sprint’s worth of planning, because there’s no sprint boundary to derail.

• Productivity is up, and it feels different. The team describes it as moving faster and being empowered to solve problems and get feedback closer to real time.

• The team likes it. That matters more than it sounds like it should. People spend more time on the work they actually enjoy and less on the “work around the work,” including status updates, backlog grooming, and re-litigating priority in a planning meeting. They feel
